documents.ai_summary: FirstLight Power, a major hydropower operator in New England, proposes amending the effective date of ISO-NE's proposed PPR reduction (from $9,337/MWh to $3,500/MWh) from September 2026 to June 2028 to protect resources that have already cleared auctions at the higher rate. The PPR is a critical two-settlement mechanism that determines performance payment/penalty rates for hydropower and other capacity resources, and FirstLight argues that retroactive changes to this rate after auctions clear violate the principle of fixed financial positions and could undermine the market's incentive structure.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing Capacity Auction Reforms with Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) that introduce Maximum Capability (MCap) and Dependable Capability (DCap) metrics to replace existing accreditation methodology. MCap represents physical supply capability irrespective of seasonality (including a Global MCap for intermittent resources), while DCap represents expected availability during seasonal peak load hours. These changes will materially affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage resources are accredited and compensated in ISO-NE's capacity market starting Q2 2027.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing new seasonal and global capability (MCap/DCap) metrics for capacity accreditation under CAR-SA reforms. The framework establishes temperature-normalized seasonal performance standards and removes certain audit requirements, which will impact how hydropower resources demonstrate their seasonal capabilities and are accredited in the capacity market. Hydropower operators will need to ensure compliance with summer (≥80°F) and winter (≤32°F) audit requirements.
